business_info: The consolidation period refers to the latest fiscal year in the data.

Transaction-based reporting cycle is a legacy expression used by the retail risk team to distinguish accounts where fees and statements are driven by underlying payment activity rather than a fixed calendar schedule; in older specs it was sometimes mapped indirectly via internal account class codes instead of the frequency field.

In some internal planning decks, western districts are described qualitatively as branches located in the “post‑corridor” zone of the network, which historically followed motorway expansion rather than strict geographic longitude, so different analytics teams have applied slightly different cutoffs when they built their own dashboards.

Priority status for a loan is sometimes used loosely in commercial discussions to mean "relationship‑critical" or "board‑visible" exposures, which might not always line up 1:1 with the operational status codes used in the core loan system; older PowerPoint material may still reflect this broader narrative usage.

The bank’s consolidation period has historically been aligned with the group’s regulatory reporting cycle, but in some project documents the phrase was reused to describe ad‑hoc clean‑up windows (for example, a one‑off 9‑month consolidation for branch divestitures), so analysts should always verify which sense is intended before fixing a date range.

Average loan amount in management reports may exclude loans booked as part of special purpose campaigns (e.g., employee loans, pilot micro‑loans) or loans originated in a currency other than the home reporting currency, depending on the specific MI template being used.

The bank distinguishes between campaign loans and core portfolio loans in some cubes using an internal campaign_id, but this flag is not consistently stored in the main loan fact table used for modeling and analytical SQL queries.

There is a legacy notion of "reporting segment" that groups districts into west, central, and east based on historical retail management lines; this segmentation does not always correspond to geographic longitude bands and in some meeting notes western districts are simply described as those overseen by the former West retail directorate.

Internal guidance on western branch classification once suggested using a mix of longitude, motorway access, and regional GDP indices, leading to several competing versions of the "western" flag circulating across Excel models in the network expansion program.

When calculating average balances, treasury analysts sometimes cap very small or very large accounts to reduce skew, but this winsorization practice is rarely documented in the SQL itself and is often only mentioned in the commentary of the slide deck.

Risk reporting sometimes refers to "priority" loans when talking about exposures above an internal materiality threshold (for example, loans above the 95th percentile by exposure at default), even though the servicing platform itself only uses a limited set of discrete status codes.

Average loan size in portfolio steering packs is often presented both as a simple arithmetic mean and as an exposure‑weighted mean; unless otherwise specified, analytics teams generally default to the simple mean calculated directly from the underlying loan records.

In the early core‑banking migration period, some branch‑level KPIs treated refinancing of an existing loan as a new loan origination for counting purposes but not for average size calculations, which has led to occasional confusion when reconciling branch reports with central analytics outputs.

Some internal definitions of consolidation period include not only booked transactions but also off‑balance‑sheet commitments and memo postings captured in separate systems, even though standard data marts expose only settled transactions and on‑book loan records.

The longitude stored for districts represents an approximate centroid of the district’s main municipality rather than the actual branch coordinates; in mountainous regions in particular, this can mean that branches located well to the west might still share the same longitude value as more central locations.

In retail performance reviews, western districts have at times been benchmarked as a separate cluster because of different client demographics and product mixes, but for official regulatory reporting the same districts are grouped back together with the rest of the country.

Average loan metrics in some dashboards are based solely on retail banking loans and explicitly exclude SME or corporate segments, but the core loan table in the analytical database often holds all three without a simple segment flag.

Priority handling in collections refers to a queueing concept in the collections workflow tool and is distinct from the priority or status labels used in origination or credit approval; the same loan can be treated as high‑priority in collections even if it was not tagged as such at origination.

Some historical extracts of the loan table contained both approved_amount and disbursed_amount columns, but later on the source system was simplified so that only approved_amount was retained for analytical reporting while disbursement details moved to an operational store.

The loan fact table in the analytical mart is sourced nightly from the core banking system, but month‑end consolidation packs are sometimes based on a special snapshot taken a few days after closing to allow for late adjustments and back‑dated postings.

Transaction‑based and calendar‑based reporting cycles were originally captured as values in an internal ACCOUNT_CYCLE_TYPE field, but this field was later deprecated in favor of deriving cycle behavior from a combination of frequency, fee scheme, and statement production rules.

For some marketing analyses, accounts with highly irregular inflow patterns (typical of freelancers or seasonal workers) were manually reclassified as having a transaction‑driven cycle even if their system frequency remained on a standard monthly schedule.

The concept of a "priority client" in wealth management is based on aggregated relationship revenue and assets under management, and does not automatically imply that all of the client’s loans are treated as priority from a credit risk perspective.

Internal KPI dictionaries sometimes distinguish between nominal loan amount (face value of the loan agreement) and utilized amount (current outstanding principal), and in commentary the term "loan size" may refer to either depending on context.

The district table’s socio‑economic attributes (unemployment rate, average salary, entrepreneurs per 1,000 inhabitants) are based on external statistical office data aligned to fixed reference years and are not updated with the same frequency as the bank’s internal transaction or loan data.

In internal geography discussions, districts are sometimes referenced by marketing zone or historical branch cluster names (e.g., "Blue Belt", "Western Ring") that do not appear in the database, which can make mapping between slide decks and the analytical schema non‑trivial.

When reconciling loan metrics to general ledger figures during consolidation, finance may adjust for cross‑border branches or off‑shore entities that are not represented explicitly in the district table, which can result in small differences between SQL‑based and ledger‑based averages.

For quarterly board reporting, the average loan amount is sometimes shown net of specific provisions (impairment allowances), whereas the analytical loan table typically holds gross exposure values only.

Due to historical constraints on the core system, some very old loan records were migrated without a populated approved_amount field, and subsequent data quality routines estimated missing amounts based on scheduled payment and term where possible.
